content: AD diagnosis uses 2018 NIA-AA Research Framework defining AD biologically via biomarkers (ATN system), independent of clinical symptoms. A (amyloid): positive amyloid PET or low CSF Aβ42 or Aβ42/40 ratio. T (tau): positive tau PET or elevated CSF phosphorylated tau (p-tau). N (neurodegeneration): structural MRI (medial temporal atrophy), FDG-PET (temporoparietal hypometabolism), or elevated CSF total tau. Clinical diagnosis: history of progressive episodic memory impairment plus deficits in other cognitive domains (language, visuospatial, executive function), functional impairment. MMSE/MoCA screen cognition; MoCA more sensitive for MCI. Neuropsychological testing characterizes pattern (hippocampal-predominant in AD). MRI excludes vascular, tumor, NPH; shows hippocampal/medial temporal atrophy. FDG-PET shows temporoparietal hypometabolism. Amyloid PET detects fibrillar Aβ. CSF biomarkers: low Aβ42, elevated p-tau and t-tau. Blood biomarkers emerging: plasma p-tau217, p-tau181, GFAP show high accuracy for AD pathology. Differential diagnosis: vascular dementia, Lewy body dementia (visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuations), frontotemporal dementia (behavioral/language predominant), reversible causes (B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, depression).
